name: Continuity and Differentiability — Smooth Curves and Their Rules
whatIsIt: A function is CONTINUOUS if its graph has no breaks, holes or jumps — you can draw it without lifting your pen. It is DIFFERENTIABLE where it is smooth (has a clear tangent slope). Class 12 adds the rules for differentiating combinations: the PRODUCT rule, the QUOTIENT rule, and the powerful CHAIN rule for a 'function inside a function'.
whyNeeded: Continuity and differentiability are the ground rules of calculus — they say where rates of change even make sense. The product, quotient and chain rules let you differentiate almost any formula in physics, economics and engineering.
realLifeUses: Modelling smooth motion (no sudden jumps in position)
Rates in economics — cost and revenue functions
Anywhere a smooth rate of change is needed

vocabulary.meaning: No breaks, holes or jumps — drawable without lifting the pen.
Smooth enough to have a single tangent slope at each point.
Differentiate the outside function, then multiply by the derivative of the inside.
d(uv) = u·(dv) + v·(du) — differentiate one, keep the other, then swap and add.

story.title: The Smooth Slide
story.text: Ishaan draws a slide with a sudden step in the middle. 'A ball can't roll smoothly over a jump,' says Sir — 'that break means the function isn't continuous there.' Ishaan redraws it smooth. 'Now it's continuous AND smooth, so it has a slope everywhere.' Sir asks for the slope rule of x³: by the power rule d/dx(x³) = 3x², which at x = 2 is 3 × 4 = 12.
story.extractedProblem: Differentiate x³ and find its value at x = 2.
story.answerInStory: 12 — d/dx(x³) = 3x², and at x = 2 that is 3 × 4 = 12.

shapes.label: smooth curve with a tangent — differentiable; a break would not be

visual.caption: A continuous, smooth curve has a clear tangent slope at each point (differentiable). A jump or sharp corner breaks that.

teachingGallery.title: Rules for slopes
teachingGallery.note: Power rule for simple terms; chain rule for a function inside a function.

examples.caption: Chain rule: outside derivative × inside derivative.
A continuous function has no jump — the graph flows through.

standardMethod.summary: Check continuity (no breaks). Differentiate with the power rule term by term; use product/quotient rules for products/ratios and the chain rule for nested functions.
steps: Continuity: is there any break, hole or jump? If not, it is continuous.
Power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹; constants have derivative 0.
Chain rule: d/dx f(g(x)) = f′(g(x)) · g′(x) (outside derivative × inside derivative).
Substitute the point to get the slope there.

workedExamples.problem: Differentiate x³ and evaluate at x = 2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Differentiating a nested function without multiplying by the inside's derivative.
commonMistakes.fix: Use the chain rule: after the outside derivative, multiply by the derivative of the inside function.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a function with a jump or sharp corner is still differentiable there.
commonMistakes.fix: No smooth tangent at a break or corner → not differentiable there. Differentiable needs continuous AND smooth.

easy.q: In d/dx(x⁵) = 5x⁴, what number comes to the front?
hintLadder: Power rule brings the exponent down.

challenge.q: A graph has a sudden jump at x = 3. There it is…

questions.q: To differentiate a function inside a function you use the…

teachBackPrompt: Draw a smooth curve and a broken one for a grown-up; explain which is continuous and differentiable. Then use the power rule to show d/dx(x³) = 3x² = 12 at x = 2, and describe the chain rule in one line.

revisionCard.summary: Continuous = no breaks (drawable without lifting the pen). Differentiable = smooth, has a tangent slope. Power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹, constants → 0. Product: u·v′ + v·u′. Chain: outside derivative × inside derivative. A break or corner is not differentiable.

rememberIt.hook: Continuous = draw without lifting the pen. Differentiable = smooth (no sharp corners)
rememberIt.unpack: Differentiable implies continuous, but NOT the reverse. A corner (like |x| at 0) is continuous but not differentiable.

studentQuestions.a: You can draw the graph without lifting your pen - no breaks, holes, or jumps.

studentQuestions.q: Can something be continuous but not differentiable?
studentQuestions.a: Yes - a sharp corner like |x| at 0. No break (continuous), but no single slope at the point (not differentiable).
